title: Oh, how the thought of God attracts
firstLine: Oh, how the thought of God attracts
publicDomainBasis: Frederick William Faber (1814–1863), James Walch (1837–1901). All public domain.

sections.label: Verse 1
sections.text:
Oh, how the thought of God attracts
And draws the heart from earth,
And sickens it of passing shows
And dissipating mirth!

sections.label: Verse 2
sections.text:
’Tis not enough to save our souls,
To shun th’ eternal fires;
The thought of God will rouse the heart
To more sublime desires.

sections.label: Verse 3
sections.text:
God only is the creature’s home,
Though rough and straight the road;
Yet nothing less can satisfy
The love that longs for God.

sections.label: Verse 4
sections.text:
Oh, utter but the Name of God
Down in your heart of hearts,
And see how from the world at once
All tempting light departs.

sections.label: Verse 5
sections.text:
A trusting heart, a yearning eye,
Can win their way above;
If mountains can be moved by faith,
Is there less power in love?

sections.label: Verse 6
sections.text:
How little of that road, my soul!
How little hast thou gone!
Take heart, and let the thought of God
Allure thee further on.

sections.label: Verse 7
sections.text:
The freedom from all willful sin,
The Christian’s daily task;
Oh! these are graces far below
What longing love would ask!

sections.label: Verse 8
Verse 9
sections.text:
Then keep thy conscience sensitive;
No inward token miss:
And go where grace entices thee—
Perfection lies in this.
